title: Emergency Care: Foreign Object Stuck in Cat's Eye
meta_desc: Suspect something is in your cat's eye? Learn why this is a high-urgency veterinary emergency and how to handle it safely.
content_html:
What is a Foreign Object in a Cat's Eye?

A foreign object refers to any external material—such as a grass seed, splinter, dust particle, or hair—that becomes lodged on the surface of the eye (cornea) or trapped under the eyelids. These objects cause significant irritation, inflammation, and potential trauma to the delicate ocular tissues.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Any suspected foreign object in the eye is considered a veterinary emergency. Ocular injuries can progress rapidly; delay in treatment can lead to corneal ulcers, secondary infections, or permanent vision loss. If your cat is squinting, pawing at their eye, or showing signs of distress, seek immediate professional care.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your emergency vet, providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected eye can be incredibly beneficial. A photo allows the triage team to assess the location of the object and the degree of inflammation before you even arrive. This helps the clinic prioritize your case and prepare the necessary ophthalmic tools for immediate extraction and stabilization.

faqs.question: Should I try to remove the object myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to remove an object from your cat's eye at home. Doing so can cause the object to penetrate deeper or lead to a rupture of the eyeball. Keep your cat from pawing at the eye and head straight to a vet.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a foreign body in the eye?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive blinking or squinting (blepharospasm), clear or colored discharge, redness, cloudiness on the eye surface, and the cat repeatedly rubbing its face against furniture or with its paws.

faqs.question: What should I do while traveling to the vet?
faqs.answer: If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), place it on your cat immediately to prevent further self-trauma. Keep the carrier dark and quiet to help reduce your cat's stress and minimize eye movement.
